intro: March 2000. You run Blockbuster. 9,000 stores, $800M/year in late fees alone, and a market cap north of $5B. A tiny DVD-mail startup in Los Gatos wants a meeting.

realOutcome.summary: Blockbuster filed for bankruptcy in 2010 and was liquidated by 2014. Every decision below is what actually happened, in order.

decisions.scenario: Reed Hastings flies to Dallas and offers to sell you Netflix for $50M, proposing to run it as your online brand. Your executives laugh him out of the room.

choices.text: Buy them. $50M is nothing to us.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Blockbuster.com launches with Netflix's DVD-mail engine years ahead of schedule. You own the category before streaming even exists.

choices.text: Pass. DVD-by-mail is a niche hobby business.
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. CEO John Antioco's team called the offer a joke. Netflix is worth roughly 400x what you offered to buy it for.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Netflix pockets your licensing fee, keeps its own brand, and builds the exact same company anyway. You bought them three years of runway.

decisions.scenario: Late fees are $800M a year — pure profit, and roughly a third of your total earnings. Customers hate them. Netflix has none.

choices.text: Kill late fees company-wide, right now.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take the earnings hit early, rebuild the brand around customer trust, and blunt Netflix's biggest talking point years before it becomes fatal.

choices.text: Keep them. It's a third of our profit.
choices.reveal: This is what happened until 2005, when mounting pressure finally forced the change. It came 5 years too late — and cost you a $630M settlement over hidden fees in the fine print.

choices.text: Rebrand them as a 'convenience fee' instead.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Customers see through the relabeling instantly. The backlash is worse than doing nothing — you've just admitted the fee was never about convenience.

decisions.scenario: 2005. Activist investor Carl Icahn joins your board and wants aggressive cost-cutting and dividends now. CEO John Antioco wants to keep investing in the online fight against Netflix. They clash publicly.

choices.text: Back Antioco. Keep funding the online war.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Sustained investment in Blockbuster Online lets you actually compete on streaming infrastructure by the time it matters, instead of gutting the team that built it.

choices.text: Back Icahn. Antioco resigns in 2007; you install Jim Keyes, a cost-cutting exec from 7-Eleven.
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. Keyes later said in 2008: 'Netflix, Redbox, and Video On Demand don't scare us.' Blockbuster filed for bankruptcy two years later.

choices.text: Force a compromise: cut costs but keep the online bet alive.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The half-measure satisfies no one. Icahn keeps pushing, the online team is starved of resources anyway, and you get the worst of both strategies.

decisions.scenario: 2007. Your 'Total Access' program — swap online rentals for in-store exchanges free — is working. Subscribers are flooding in and Netflix's own growth is stalling for the first time. But Total Access is bleeding cash and Wall Street is furious about the margin hit.

choices.text: Double down. Pour more marketing into Total Access.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Reed Hastings later admitted Total Access was 'the single biggest threat' Netflix ever faced. Pressing the advantage here could have ended the war in 2007.

choices.text: Pull back. Wall Street wants margins, not growth.
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. Blockbuster scaled back Total Access marketing in late 2007 to protect short-term earnings, right as Netflix was most vulnerable. Netflix's stock had cratered 75% — then fully recovered within a year.

choices.text: Spin Total Access off as a separate company.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The spinoff fragments your only real advantage, splits the customer base, and hands Netflix an easier, more confused competitor to beat.

decisions.scenario: 2010. Store traffic is collapsing, debt is due, and streaming has arrived. Dish Network is circling as a potential buyer if you go through bankruptcy.

choices.text: Pivot hard to streaming before the cash runs out.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A real streaming bet in 2010, backed by your remaining brand recognition and store footprint for hybrid returns, gives you a fighting chance against a Netflix that hadn't yet locked up the market.

choices.text: File for Chapter 11 and hope for a buyer.
choices.reveal: This is exactly what happened. Blockbuster filed for bankruptcy in September 2010. Dish Network bought the remains out of bankruptcy in 2011 for $320M — less than 1% of your peak valuation.

choices.text: Sell the DVD library and go all-in on real estate.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You're now a mid-size commercial landlord with an expiring lease book and no media business. Technically solvent longer, but there's no company left worth running.

bands.line: You out-executed history. Villain episode: WON.
Limping but alive. Dish buys you anyway, just later and for more.
Faithful to history. That's the problem. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Netflix 400x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company at a similar crossroads →
